title: The US paper fleet: The Jones Act and the 2026 Iran war stress test
gist: The 2026 Iran war forced the White House to waive the Jones Act within weeks because the US-flag fleet had collapsed to 93 ships and could not move America's own oil and LNG between its coasts; the authors argue for scrapping the domestic-build requirement while keeping US-flag, crew, and ownership mandates.

themes.theme: Jones Act failure in the Iran crisis

insights.claim: The Strait of Hormuz is a chokepoint for about 20 percent of global oil supplies and 30 percent of global LNG supplies, making its closure exactly the kind of emergency the Jones Act was designed to weather.
insights.speaker: Atlantic Council
insights.so_what: Hormuz concentration in LNG (30 percent) exceeds its oil share, making gas markets even more chokepoint-exposed than crude.
insights.locator: The closure of the Strait of Hormuz

insights.claim: Because there were essentially no Jones Act-compliant LNG carriers, it is impossible to ship America's abundant Gulf Coast natural gas to New England, leaving US regions reliant on foreign fuel imports even as the US leads the world in energy exports.
insights.speaker: Atlantic Council
insights.so_what: US energy self-sufficiency is undermined domestically by its own shipping law, a persistent structural mispricing between US Gulf and coastal fuel markets.
insights.locator: A glaring failure was in LNG

insights.claim: Faced with catastrophic supply-shortage risk as tankers were diverted to more lucrative international routes, the Department of Homeland Security issued a broad multi-month waiver of the Jones Act on March 17, 2026.
insights.speaker: Atlantic Council
insights.so_what: The government suspending its own maritime-security law under stress is a de facto admission the statute is a liability, strengthening the reform case.

insights.claim: The 2026 stress test demonstrated that the Jones Act created a paradoxical system that is both economically burdensome in peacetime and operationally brittle in crisis.
insights.speaker: Atlantic Council
insights.so_what: Framing the law as failing on both cost and security grounds is the core investment-relevant thesis for maritime policy reform.

insights.claim: Jones Act-eligible oceangoing vessels collapsed from 434 in 1950 to just 93 ships in 2026, even as the American economy grew tenfold.
insights.speaker: Atlantic Council
insights.so_what: A 78 percent fleet shrinkage against a 10x larger economy quantifies the hollowing out of US sealift capacity.

insights.claim: The Ready Reserve Force surge sealift fleet is 45 ships with an average age over 45 years, more than double the UNCTAD global average of just over 22 years and above the typical 25-30 year scrappage age.
insights.speaker: Atlantic Council
insights.so_what: A geriatric reserve fleet is a material military readiness gap and a driver of the Pentagon's used-ship buying program.

insights.claim: In a 2019 stress test the Ready Reserve Force achieved a mission success rate of just 41 percent against an 85 percent target.
insights.speaker: Atlantic Council
insights.so_what: A sub-50 percent readiness score exposes the fragility of US surge sealift for any major ground deployment.
insights.locator: The consequences of this neglect became obvious

insights.claim: The US faces a critical shortfall of over 1,800 mariners needed to sustain major sealift operations while producing a minuscule 0.1 percent of the world's commercial ships, versus Chinese shipbuilding capacity 232 times greater.
insights.speaker: Atlantic Council
insights.so_what: A 232x Chinese shipbuilding advantage plus a mariner shortage frames the US maritime-industrial base as a strategic vulnerability versus China.
insights.locator: The decay extends beyond the ships

insights.claim: During Operations Desert Shield and Desert Storm, only eight of 281 Ready Reserve Force and commercial ships chartered by Military Sealift Command were Jones Act-eligible, with foreign-flagged vessels carrying 27 percent of dry cargo versus 13 percent for US-flagged commercial vessels.
insights.speaker: Atlantic Council
insights.so_what: The Jones Act fleet has historically been marginal to actual US wartime sealift, undercutting its primary national-security justification.
insights.quote: It would have taken us three more months to complete the sealift ourselves.
insights.locator: In addition to its failure to ensure

insights.claim: Doubling down on the US-build requirement is fiscally unattainable because US shipyards charge up to five times the price of their foreign competitors, making full domestic recapitalization of the fleet unaffordable.
insights.speaker: Atlantic Council
insights.so_what: A 5x domestic price premium makes reshoring commercial shipbuilding economically implausible without massive subsidies.
insights.locator: The first path is to double down

insights.claim: The Pentagon's Vessel Acquisition Management program, which buys used foreign-built ships to plug Ready Reserve Force gaps, sends US taxpayer dollars to foreign shipping conglomerates that then use the capital to build new ships in Chinese and South Korean yards.
insights.speaker: Atlantic Council
insights.so_what: Current stopgap policy inadvertently finances Chinese and South Korean shipbuilders, the very competitors it seeks to counter.
insights.locator: The second path is the one the Pentagon

insights.claim: The authors argue US maritime policy should eliminate the Jones Act's US-build requirement while retaining its US-flag, crew, and ownership mandates, letting US carriers buy modern affordable ships on the global market to recapitalize the fleet and create demand for mariners.
insights.speaker: Atlantic Council
insights.so_what: Dropping only the build rule is the pragmatic reform path; watch whether Congress moves this way after the waiver expires.
insights.locator: This requires eliminating the Jones Act's

insights.claim: The waiver's modest effect on gasoline prices confirmed that domestic shipping costs, while real, are a minor driver of fuel prices compared to global crude markets.
insights.speaker: Atlantic Council
insights.so_what: Jones Act reform is more a supply-resilience and industrial-base issue than a consumer fuel-price lever.
insights.locator: The waiver's modest effects on gasoline prices

insights.claim: Whether Congress uses the 150-day waiver experiment as evidence for structural reform or lets the waiver expire in August 2026 with the law resuming unchanged will determine the durability of the Jones Act.
insights.speaker: Atlantic Council
insights.so_what: August 2026 is the key policy catalyst; the outcome shapes long-term US shipbuilding and shipping economics.
